### 1. Task outcome (short version):
Manash Pratim Kashyap authored a study on customer retention titled "An Empirical Study on Brand Switching Behavior of Electronic Goods: A Markovian Approach", published in July 2018.

### 2. Task outcome (extremely detailed version):
The study titled "An Empirical Study on Brand Switching Behavior of Electronic Goods: A Markovian Approach" was conducted by Manash Pratim Kashyap, co-authored with Dhiraj Neog and Pallabi Sinha. It was published in the Research Journal of Social and Life Sciences in July 2018. This empirical research focuses on customer retention in the context of electronic goods, analyzing various factors that influence brand switching behavior. 

Using a Markovian approach, the research quantitatively models brand switching scenarios among consumers, assessing how likely consumers are to switch brands over time and identifying the key determinants of customer loyalty. This methodology is beneficial for understanding consumer behavior trends in an increasingly competitive market for electronic goods, offering vital insights for formulating effective marketing retention strategies.

### 3. Additional context (if relevant):
The research highlights the significance of consumer loyalty within the electronic goods industry, particularly due to the rapid pace of innovation and competition. By utilizing mathematical models in assessing brand loyalty, it contributes to the broader understanding of consumer decision-making processes and aids organizations in enhancing their customer retention strategies.

Fader's studies: Here is the final answer from your managed agent 'search_agent':
### 1. Task outcome (short version):
Peter S. Fader has published studies related to customer retention during the years 2018-2019, notably including the paper titled "How to Project Customer Retention Revisited: The Role of Duration Dependence."

### 2. Task outcome (extremely detailed version):
In the publication "How to Project Customer Retention Revisited," Fader and his colleagues present an extension of the beta-geometric (BG) distribution model, now integrating the beta-discrete-Weibull (BdW) distribution. This new model captures the dynamics of individual churn probabilities, diverging from the traditional view that customer retention improves over time purely due to cross-sectional differences. Key findings and insights from this study include:
  - Cohort-level retention rates generally increase over time, often due to cross-sectional heterogeneity rather than a decline in churn propensity among individuals.
  - The BdW distribution can accommodate not just increasing retention patterns but also complex dynamics like initial declines in retention before increases (U-shaped retention curves).
  - It challenges the conventional wisdom that an individual’s propensity to churn decreases with tenure, finding instead that churn probabilities may increase over time.
  - There are practical implications for businesses, suggesting that focusing on cross-sectional heterogeneity is more critical than assuming time-dependent dynamics in customer churn.
This research emphasizes the need for businesses to adopt models that reflect variability in customer behavior more accurately.

### 3. Additional context (if relevant):
Other papers authored by PS Fader during this period may also exist but would require further searching in academic databases to gather comprehensive insights on his contributions to customer retention strategies during 2018-2019.

[{'type': 'text', 'text': 'Observation:\nAddress: https://papers.ssrn.com/sol3/papers.cfm?abstract_id=3100346\nTitle: Error 403\nViewport position: Showing page 1 of 1.\n=======================\n## Error 403\n\n\n\nEnable JavaScript and cookies to continue\nAddress: file:///home/jerry/gaia_agents/smolagents/examples/open_deep_research/downloads_folder/BdW_JIM_2018-01-10__22.pdf\nViewport position: Showing page 1 of 19.\n=======================\n“How to Project Customer Retention” Revisited:\nThe Role of Duration Dependence\n\nPeter S. Fader\nBruce G. S. Hardie\nYuzhou Liu\nJoseph Davin\nThomas Steenburgh†\n\nJanuary 2018\n\n†Peter S. Fader is the Frances and Pei-Yuan Chia Professor of Marketing at The Wharton School of\nthe University of Pennsylvania (address: 771 Jon M. Huntsman Hall, 3730 Walnut Street, Philadelphia,\nPA 19104-6340; phone: (215) 898-1132; email: faderp@wharton.upenn.edu; web: www.petefader.com).\nBruce G. S. Hardie is Professor of Marketing at the London Business School (email: bhardie@london.edu;\nweb: www.brucehardie.com). Yuzhou Liu is a Quantitative Researcher at Man Numeric. Joseph Davin is\nHead of Data Science, Two Six Capital. Thomas Steenburgh is the Bank of America Research Professor\nof Business Administration at the Darden Graduate School of Business, University of Virginia.\n\n\x0cAbstract\n\n“How to Project Customer Retention” Revisited:\nThe Role of Duration Dependence\n\nCohort-level retention rates typically increase over time, and the beta-geometric (BG) distribu-\ntion has proven to be a robust model for capturing and projecting these patterns into the future.\nAccording to this model, the phenomenon of increasing cohort-level retention rates is purely due\nto cross-sectional heterogeneity; an individual customer’s propensity to churn does not change\nover time. In this paper we present the beta-discrete-Weibull (BdW) distribution as an exten-\nsion to the BG model, one that allows individual-level churn probabilities to increase or decrease\nover time. In addition to capturing the phenomenon of increasing cohort-level retention rates,\nthis new model can also accommodate situations in which there is an initial dip in retention\nrates before they increase (i.e., a U-shaped cohort-level retention curve). A key ﬁnding is that\neven when aggregate retention rates are monotonically increasing, the individual-level churn\nprobabilities are unlikely to be declining over time, as conventional wisdom would suggest. We\ncarefully explore these connections between heterogeneity, duration dependence, and the shape\nof the retention curve, and draw some managerially relevant conclusions, e.g., that account-\ning for cross-sectional heterogeneity is more important than accounting for any individual-level\ndynamics in churn propensities.\n\nKeywords: beta-geometric (BG) distribution, beta-discrete-Weibull (BdW) distribu-\n\ntion, retention rate dynamics.\n\n\x0c1\n\nIntroduction\n\nAny researcher working with data from a business that has a “contractual” relationship with its\n\ncustomers (e.g., one with a subscription-based business model) will want models for projecting\n\ncustomer retention (or equivalently, tenure) as part of their toolkit. For example, estimates of\n\nthe length of a customer’s relationship with the ﬁrm lie at the heart of any attempt to compute\n\ncustomer lifetime value (CLV). Similarly, such models are useful when evaluating the relative\n\nperformance of diﬀerent acquisition channels.\n\nFader and Hardie (2007), hereafter FH, presented the beta-geometric (BG) distribution as a\n\nsimple probability model for projecting customer retention. This model is based on an easy-to-\n\nunderstand “story” of customer behavior, is simple to implement (e.g., can be done so in Excel),\n\nand its estimates of customer retention over a longitudinal holdout period have proven to be\n\nsurprisingly accurate and robust.\n\nAccording to this model, the widely observed phenomenon of increasing cohort-level retention\n\nrates (Reichheld 1996) is purely due to cross-sectional heterogeneity, with individual customers\n\nhaving a constant propensity to churn. Cohort-level retention rates increase because those\n\ncustomers with high churn propensities drop out early on, leaving an ever-increasing proportion\n\nof customers who have low propensities to churn. This assumption that an individual customer’s\n\npropensity to churn does not change over time ﬂies in the face of conventional wisdom, which\n\nassumes that a customer’s propensity to churn decreases the longer their tenure with the ﬁrm.\n\nWhile cohort-level retention rates are typically monotonically increasing with tenure, we\n\nsometimes observe an initial dip before they increase (e.g., Israel 2005, Nitzan et al. 2011, and\n\none of the FH datasets), a phenomenon that the BG model cannot capture. In this paper we\n\ndevelop a generalization of the BG model, one that both relaxes the assumption of time-invariant\n\nindividual-level propensities to churn and is suﬃciently ﬂexible to capture the phenomenon of\n\nnon-monotonically increasing cohort-level retention rates. Surprisingly, we ﬁnd that when the\n\nassumption of constant individual-level propensities to churn is violated, it is more likely that\n\nthese propensities increase with tenure (rather than decrease, as conventional wisdom would\n\nsuggest).\n\nThis paper is organized as follows.\n\nIn the next section we re-examine the work of FH,\n\n1\n\n\x0creviewing the BG model and its empirical performance. We then present our generalization\n\nof the BG model, the beta-discrete-Weibull (BdW) distribution, and examine its performance\n\nusing the'}]

It is clear that while both studies use different models, the terms "Markovian Approach" and the essence of **duration dependence** in retention can be linked. However, "Markovian" is a specific term more related to modeling practices rather than a general two-word model type used by both.
